summary: When the assembly line stalls — and how CPUs recover.

summary: Writing, compiling, and running your very first C program.

summary: Named containers that hold data — and the memory behind them.

summary: Variables that store memory addresses — the 'GPS coordinates' of data.

title: Arrays
summary: Contiguous blocks of memory — like numbered shelves in a warehouse.

title: Recursion
summary: Functions that call themselves — like Russian nesting dolls.

title: Stacks
summary: A last-in, first-out structure — like a stack of plates.

title: Queues
summary: A first-in, first-out structure — like a grocery store checkout line.

summary: Nodes connected by pointers — like a treasure hunt with clues.

title: Trees & Binary Trees
summary: Hierarchical structures branching from a single root — like a family tree.

summary: Trees that keep data sorted for lightning-fast lookups.

title: Binary Heaps
summary: A tournament bracket structure that always knows the winner.

title: Hash Tables

summary: Nodes and edges representing connections — like a metro map.

title: B-Trees & B+ Trees
summary: Wide, balanced trees optimized for disk storage — like an encyclopedia index.

summary: A step-by-step recipe for solving a problem.

title: Big-O Notation
summary: A way to measure how much slower an algorithm gets as input grows.

title: Searching Algorithms
summary: Finding a needle in a haystack — linear search vs. binary search.

title: Sorting Algorithms
summary: Putting things in order — from bubble sort to merge sort.

title: Hashing
summary: Turning data into fixed-size fingerprints for instant retrieval.

title: Divide & Conquer
summary: Breaking big problems into smaller identical sub-problems.

title: Greedy Algorithms
summary: Making the locally optimal choice at every step.

title: Dynamic Programming
summary: Solving problems by remembering the answers to sub-problems.

title: Graph Traversals: BFS & DFS
summary: Systematically visiting every node in a graph.

title: Minimum Spanning Trees
summary: Connecting all nodes with the least total cost.

summary: Finding the cheapest route between two nodes in a graph.

title: What Can Machines Compute?
summary: The fundamental limits of what any computer can ever solve.

title: Regular Expressions & Finite Automata
summary: Pattern-matching machines with no memory beyond their current state.

title: Context-Free Grammars & Push-Down Automata
summary: Machines that understand nested structure — like matching parentheses.

title: The Pumping Lemma
summary: A proof technique for showing what finite automata cannot recognize.

title: Turing Machines
summary: The most powerful theoretical computer — an infinite tape with a read/write head.

title: Decidability & Undecidability
summary: Problems that no algorithm can ever solve — provably impossible tasks.

title: Complexity Classes (P vs NP)
summary: Classifying problems by how hard they are to solve vs. verify.

title: The Big Picture: From English to Electricity
summary: How human-readable code becomes machine instructions a CPU executes.

title: Lexical Analysis (Tokenization)
summary: Breaking source code into tokens — the words of a programming language.

title: Parsing & Syntax Trees
summary: Building a tree structure that represents the grammar of your code.

title: Syntax-Directed Translation
summary: Attaching meaning to grammar rules to generate intermediate code.

title: Runtime Environments
summary: How the compiled program manages memory, stack frames, and function calls.

title: Intermediate Code Generation
summary: Translating the syntax tree into a platform-independent instruction set.

title: Optimization & Data Flow Analysis
summary: Making generated code faster and smaller without changing its behavior.

summary: The kitchen manager that coordinates all hardware and software resources.

title: CPU Scheduling
summary: Deciding which process gets the CPU next — and for how long.

summary: The shared knife problem — coordinating access to shared resources.

title: Deadlock
summary: When processes wait forever for each other — a circular standoff.

summary: How separate processes share data — pipes, shared memory, and message passing.

title: Memory Management & Virtual Memory
summary: Giving every process the illusion of having all memory to itself.

title: File Systems
summary: Organizing data on disk into files and directories you can find again.

title: I/O Scheduling
summary: Optimizing the order of disk and device requests.

title: System Calls & User/Kernel Space
summary: The controlled doorway between your program and the operating system.

title: Why Databases?
summary: A filing cabinet of sorted folders — structured, searchable, reliable storage.

summary: Drawing the blueprint of your data before building the database.

summary: The language you use to ask questions of a relational database.

summary: Rules that keep your data consistent and free from redundancy.

summary: The tree structure that makes database lookups blazingly fast.

summary: How database files are physically stored and indexed on disk.

summary: All-or-nothing operations that keep your database trustworthy.

summary: Letting multiple users query the database without stepping on each other.

summary: How data travels across the world in layers — like mail through a postal system.

summary: Algorithms that guide packets through the network to their destination.

summary: Reliable vs. fast delivery — choosing how data gets there.

summary: Preventing network traffic jams and ensuring fair bandwidth sharing.

summary: The protocols that power the web, email, and file transfers.
